question: What problem do Branded Types solve?

options.text: They prevent confusion between values of the same primitive type (e.g., Email and UserId are both strings)
They speed up program execution through type optimizations
options.explanation: Branded Types exist only at the TypeScript type system level and have no impact on runtime performance

options.text: They allow storing values of different types in a single array
options.explanation: Union types or generics are used for that, not branded types

options.text: They replace the need for data validation
options.explanation: Branded Types complement validation — constructor functions typically include validation before casting to a branded type

question: What happens if a type guard function returns boolean instead of the predicate `x is Type`?

options.text: The function works at runtime, but TypeScript won't narrow the variable's type in conditional blocks
TypeScript will throw a compilation error
options.explanation: The code compiles without errors — the function simply returns boolean. But TypeScript won't automatically narrow the type in if/else

options.text: The type will be narrowed, but only inside switch/case
options.explanation: Without the `x is Type` predicate, type narrowing won't occur in if/else or switch/case

options.text: The program will crash at runtime
options.explanation: At runtime, a type guard with boolean works exactly the same — this is purely a compile-time distinction

question: What is the purpose of the `const _never: never = value` pattern in the default branch of a switch?

options.text: To handle unexpected values at runtime
options.explanation: This pattern works at compile time — it forces TypeScript to verify that all union variants are handled

options.text: To guarantee at compile time that all discriminated union variants are handled (exhaustive check)
To optimize memory when working with union types
options.explanation: The never type has no runtime impact — it's purely a type system tool

options.text: To create a breakpoint for debugging
options.explanation: Exhaustive check is a compile-time completeness verification, not a debugging tool

question: Which of the following are built-in type guards in TypeScript?

options.text: typeof x === 'string'
x instanceof Error
'key' in obj
x as Type
options.explanation: The as operator is a type assertion (type cast), not a type guard. It doesn't perform a runtime check

options.text: x === null
options.explanation: Checking for null/undefined is narrowing, but not a type guard in the classic sense. Type guards are typeof, instanceof, in, and custom predicates

question: Why is `as` used in a branded type constructor?

options.text: Because TypeScript cannot automatically cast string to Brand<string, 'Email'>, and `as` is the only way to create a branded value after validation
To improve performance when creating branded values
options.explanation: The as operator doesn't exist at runtime and has no performance impact — it's purely a compile-time operation

options.text: Because without `as` the object would be immutable
options.explanation: The as operator doesn't affect mutability. Use readonly for immutability

options.text: It's a temporary workaround that will be fixed in future TypeScript versions
options.explanation: This is intentional type system behavior — branded types by definition require explicit casting since the brand doesn't exist at runtime
